1

Accounting Basics

The two financial statements that drive everything

Section 1 — Accounting Basics

Income Statement

Measures profitability over a period of time (month, quarter, year)

Line ItemAccount
Gross Sales (Revenue)4105
− Product Cost (COGS)5105
= Gross Profit
− Operating Expenses
= Net Income
Key idea: The Income Statement resets each period. It answers: "Did we make money?"
Section 1 — Accounting Basics

Balance Sheet

A snapshot of what the company owns and owes at a single point in time

Assets (what we own)

Accounts Receivable1110
Inventory1210
Equipment, Property…

Liabilities (what we owe)

Accounts Payable2010
Loans, Accrued Exp…

Equity

Owner's Equity / Retained Earnings
Assets = Liabilities + Equity  ←  Always.
Section 1 — Accounting Basics

How Westfield Uses These

Income Statement

  • Track revenue from each sales channel
  • Monitor COGS to protect margins
  • Monthly P&L reviews by brand/class
  • Budget vs. actuals comparison

Balance Sheet

  • Monitor inventory levels (1210)
  • Track what customers owe us (1110)
  • Track what we owe vendors (2010)
  • Overall financial health snapshot
Why it matters: Every transaction you enter in NetSuite feeds into one or both of these reports. Understanding them helps you understand why NetSuite works the way it does.
2

Balanced Entries

Every transaction must balance — debits equal credits

Section 2 — Balanced Entries

The Golden Rule of Accounting

For every transaction, the total debits must equal the total credits. NetSuite enforces this automatically.

Debit Side
+
Increases
−
Decreases
=
Credit Side
−
Decreases
+
Increases
Remember: Assets & Expenses increase with debits. Revenue, Liabilities & Equity increase with credits.
Section 2 — Balanced Entries

Example: Selling a Chair for $100

When we invoice a customer, NetSuite creates balanced journal entries behind the scenes:

Revenue Recognition Entry

AccountDebitCredit
1110 Accounts Receivable$100
4105 Gross Sales$100

COGS Entry (if item costs $40)

AccountDebitCredit
5105 Product (COGS)$40
1210 Inventory$40
Section 2 — Balanced Entries

What This Means for You

  • You don't create journal entries manually — NetSuite does it when you enter transactions (invoices, bills, receipts)
  • But understanding them helps — when something looks wrong in a report, you'll know which accounts to check
  • NetSuite won't save an unbalanced transaction — if you get a "transaction is not in balance" error, check your amounts
You enter the business event. NetSuite handles the accounting math.
3

Records vs Transactions

Permanent data vs. actions that move money

Section 3 — Records vs Transactions

Two Types of Data in NetSuite

📋 Records

Permanent reference data — the "nouns"

  • Customers
  • Items / Products
  • Vendors
  • Employees
  • Locations
Records exist. They don't affect financials by themselves.

⚡ Transactions

Actions that move money — the "verbs"

  • Sales Orders
  • Invoices
  • Purchase Orders
  • Item Receipts
  • Vendor Bills
Transactions happen. They create journal entries and affect financials.
Section 3 — Records vs Transactions

How They Work Together

Transactions always reference records. Records provide the data; transactions create the accounting.

Customer Record
Item Record
Location
↓   referenced by   ↓
Sales Order Transaction
↓   generates   ↓
Journal Entry (Accounting Impact)
Think of it this way: A Customer record is like a contact card. A Sales Order is the action of selling them something.
4

Transaction Flows

How purchasing, sales, and inventory move through NetSuite

Section 4 — Transaction Flows

Purchasing Flow

From ordering to payment

Purchase Order
→
Item Receipt
→
Bill Payment

Purchase Order

No accounting impact — just a commitment to buy. Reserves budget but doesn't hit the books.

Item Receipt

This is where inventory increases.
Debit 1210 Inventory
Credit 2010 Accounts Payable

Bill Payment

When we actually pay the vendor.
Debit 2010 Accounts Payable
Credit Cash/Bank Account

Section 4 — Transaction Flows

Sales Flow

From customer order to revenue recognition

Sales Order
→
Fulfillment
→
Invoice

Sales Order

No accounting impact — records the customer's order and commits inventory

Fulfillment

Inventory decreases:
Debit 5105 COGS
Credit 1210 Inventory

Invoice

Revenue recognized:
Debit 1110 AR
Credit 4105 Gross Sales

Section 4 — Transaction Flows

Transfers, Returns & Adjustments

Inventory Transfer

Moves stock between locations

Debit 1210 Inv (To)
Credit 1210 Inv (From)

Total inventory stays the same — just moves between warehouses.

Return (RMA)

Customer returns product

Reverses the original sale: reduces AR, adds back inventory, reverses COGS

Creates a Credit Memo or Return Authorization.

Inventory Adjustment

Corrects quantity discrepancies

Debit/Credit 1210 Inv
Offset to 6820 Inv Adj

Used after physical counts or damage write-offs.

Section 4 — Transaction Flows

The Big Picture

How purchasing and sales connect through inventory

Purchasing Side
PO
→
Item Receipt
→
Vendor Bill
→
Payment
↓
1210  Inventory
↓
Sales Side
Sales Order
→
Fulfillment
→
Invoice
→
Payment
Inventory is the bridge between purchasing and sales.
Section 4 — Transaction Flows

NetSuite Transaction View

Here's what a typical transaction looks like in the system

🔲 [INSERT NETSUITE SCREENSHOT — Sales Order or Invoice form view]
Notice: The header contains customer/date info. The line items contain products, quantities, and amounts. The GL Impact tab shows the journal entries NetSuite creates.
5

Item Records

The building blocks of everything we buy and sell

Section 5 — Item Records

What's on an Item Record?

FieldDescription
Item Name / SKUUnique identifier (e.g., GCI-39154)
Display NameWhat appears on invoices and reports
DescriptionDetailed product description
ClassBrand or product line (e.g., GCI Outdoor, Westfield)
CategoryProduct type (e.g., Chairs, Wagons, Tents)
Base PriceStandard selling price
🔲 [INSERT NETSUITE SCREENSHOT — Item Record form]
Section 5 — Item Records

Cost Concepts

Understanding the different cost fields on an item

Purchase Price

— what we pay the vendor

Set on the PO. Can vary by vendor or order.

Average Cost

— what NetSuite uses for COGS

Calculated automatically. Weighted average of all purchase prices for units in stock.

Transfer Cost

— intercompany pricing

Used when transferring items between subsidiaries or internal divisions. May include markup.

Section 5 — Item Records

How Average Cost Works

NetSuite recalculates average cost with each receipt

EventQtyUnit CostOn HandAvg Cost
Receipt #1100$10.00100$10.00
Receipt #2100$12.00200$11.00
Sell 50 units−50—150$11.00
Receipt #350$14.00200$11.75
Why this matters: When we fulfill an order, NetSuite uses the current average cost to calculate COGS (5105). A higher average cost = lower margin on each sale.
Section 5 — Item Records

Item Record in NetSuite

Key tabs and fields to know

🔲 [INSERT NETSUITE SCREENSHOT — Item record showing Class, Category, Cost fields]
  • Primary Info tab: Name, SKU, description, class, category
  • Purchasing tab: Preferred vendor, purchase price
  • Pricing tab: Base price, price levels by customer
6

Customer Records

Managing who we sell to and how

Section 6 — Customer Records

Customer Record — Required Fields

FieldExample
Company NameBass Pro Shops
Customer IDAuto-generated
CategoryRetail / Mass / Direct
TermsNet 30, Net 60
Price LevelWholesale, Retail
Billing AddressRequired for invoicing
Shipping AddressCan have multiple
Auto-Population: Once a customer record exists, their terms, price level, and addresses auto-fill on new Sales Orders — saving time and reducing errors.
🔲 [INSERT NETSUITE SCREENSHOT — Customer record]
Section 6 — Customer Records

Parent / Child Customers

Large retailers often have multiple "ship-to" locations under one parent account

Bass Pro Shops (Parent)
↓
Store #0001
Springfield, MO
↓
Store #0142
Nashville, TN
↓
Store #0305
Grapevine, TX
↓
Cabela's (Child)
Sidney, NE
Parent record holds shared settings: terms, price level, credit limit, tax status
Child records inherit parent settings but have their own shipping addresses and contacts
Section 6 — Customer Records

Auto-Population in Action

When you select a customer on a Sales Order, NetSuite fills in:

Payment Terms
Price Level
Tax Status
Billing Address
Shipping Address
Sales Rep
Currency
Why this matters: Clean, complete customer records = fewer errors on orders. If a customer's terms are wrong on the record, every order will inherit that mistake.
Good data in → Good data out. Keep customer records accurate.
7

Supporting Records

Vendors, Employees, and Partners

Section 7 — Supporting Records

Other Key Record Types

🏭 Vendor Records

  • Company name & contacts
  • Payment terms
  • Currency & tax info
  • Purchase price lists
  • Lead time data

Referenced on POs and Vendor Bills

👤 Employee Records

  • Name & department
  • Role & supervisor
  • Expense report approver
  • System access level
  • Location assignment

Controls what you can see and do in NetSuite

🤝 Partner Records

  • Sales reps & agents
  • Commission rates
  • Territory assignment
  • Performance tracking

Used for commission calculations and partner portals

Section 7 — Supporting Records

How Supporting Records Connect

Every transaction references multiple record types

👤 Employee
enters the order
🏭 Vendor
supplied the product
🤝 Partner
earns commission
↓   ↓   ↓
Sales Order
↑
🛒 Customer
who's buying
📦 Items
what's being sold
Everything connects: A single Sales Order can reference a customer, multiple items, an employee, a partner, and ultimately trace back to the vendor who supplied the product.
8

System Structure

Departments, Classes, Categories, and Locations

Section 8 — System Structure

Departments

Organizational units that help segment reporting

What Departments Do

  • Segment financial reports by team
  • Track revenue & expenses per group
  • Control approvals and workflows
  • Filter dashboards and saved searches

Example Departments

  • Sales
  • Product Design & Development
  • Operations / Warehouse
  • Marketing
  • Finance / Accounting
Set on transactions and employee records. When you enter a PO, the department tells NetSuite which team is spending the money.
Section 8 — System Structure

Classes (Brands)

At Westfield, we use the Class field to track brands

GCI Outdoor
Westfield
Timber Ridge
Other Brands
On Item Records: Every product is assigned to a class/brand. This lets us run P&L reports by brand.
On Transactions: When you sell a GCI chair, the class flows through to revenue and COGS reports automatically.
Class = Brand → "How is GCI performing vs Westfield this quarter?"
Section 8 — System Structure

Item Categories

Product types that help organize inventory and reporting

🪑
Chairs
🛒
Wagons
⛺
Tents
🎒
Accessories
🔫
Gun Cases
📦
Other
Class vs Category: Class = Brand (who makes it). Category = Product type (what it is). An item like a GCI Freestyle Rocker would be Class: GCI Outdoor, Category: Chairs.
Section 8 — System Structure

Locations

Physical or logical places where inventory is stored and transactions occur

What Locations Track

  • Inventory quantities per location
  • Fulfillment origin for shipping
  • Cost tracking by warehouse
  • Transfer orders between locations

Example Locations

  • Indianapolis HQ / Warehouse
  • 3PL Warehouse (East)
  • 3PL Warehouse (West)
  • In-Transit
On every transaction: Location determines where inventory is added (receipts) or removed (fulfillments). The same item can have different quantities at different locations.
Section 8 — System Structure

How It All Connects

Every transaction carries these classification fields

Department
(who)
Class / Brand
(which brand)
Category
(what type)
Location
(where)
↓   ↓   ↓   ↓
Transaction
↓
Income Statement
filtered by all 4
Balance Sheet
filtered by all 4
These four dimensions let you slice financials any way you need:
"GCI Outdoor chairs sold from Indianapolis in Q1" ← one report
9

Final Walkthrough

Putting it all together with a real example

Section 9 — Final Walkthrough

Scenario: Bass Pro Shops Orders Chairs

Customer: Bass Pro Shops (Parent) Ship To: Store #0142 — Nashville, TN (Child) Item: GCI Freestyle Rocker (SKU: GCI-39154) Quantity: 200 units Unit Price: $35.00 (wholesale) Average Cost: $18.50 Class: GCI Outdoor Category: Chairs Location: Indianapolis Warehouse
Let's walk through the three steps: Sales Order → Fulfillment → Invoice — and see exactly what happens in the accounts.
Section 9 — Final Walkthrough

Step 1: Sales Order

Sales Order ←
→
Fulfillment
→
Invoice

What happens:

  • Order is entered with customer, items, quantities
  • Customer fields auto-populate (terms, price, address)
  • 200 units committed from Indianapolis inventory
  • Order status: Pending Fulfillment

Accounting impact:

None.
The Sales Order is a commitment, not a financial event. No journal entries are created.
Section 9 — Final Walkthrough

Step 2: Item Fulfillment

Sales Order
→
Fulfillment ←
→
Invoice

What happens:

200 chairs are picked, packed, and shipped from Indianapolis to Nashville

Accounting Entry — COGS Recognition

AccountDebitCredit
5105 Product (COGS)$3,700
1210 Inventory$3,700

200 units × $18.50 avg cost = $3,700

Inventory at Indianapolis decreases by 200 units. COGS increases by $3,700.
Section 9 — Final Walkthrough

Step 3: Invoice

Sales Order
→
Fulfillment
→
Invoice ←

What happens:

Revenue is recognized. Bass Pro now owes us $7,000.

Accounting Entry — Revenue Recognition

AccountDebitCredit
1110 Accounts Receivable$7,000
4105 Gross Sales$7,000

200 units × $35.00 sell price = $7,000

AR increases by $7,000 (Bass Pro owes us). Revenue increases by $7,000 on the Income Statement.
Section 9 — Final Walkthrough

The Complete Picture

Everything that happened across both financial statements

Income Statement Impact

4105 Gross Sales$7,000
5105 COGS($3,700)
Gross Profit$3,300

Margin: 47.1%

Balance Sheet Impact

1110 AR+$7,000
1210 Inventory−$3,700

Net assets increased by $3,300 (the profit)

Three transactions. Two financial statements updated. All balanced. All automatic.

Key Takeaways

  • Every transaction creates balanced journal entries automatically
  • Records are your reference data; Transactions are your actions
  • Inventory is the bridge between purchasing and sales
  • Department, Class, Category, Location = your reporting dimensions
  • Clean data in records = accurate financials downstream
Questions? Reach out to your manager or the accounting team.
Quick Reference

Account Number Reference

Account #NameStatement
1110Accounts ReceivableBalance Sheet
1210InventoryBalance Sheet
2010Accounts PayableBalance Sheet
4105Gross SalesIncome Statement
5105Product (COGS)Income Statement
6820Inventory AdjustmentIncome Statement
